summary: This excerpt from the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy explores the 'Socratic problem' of reconstructing the historical Socrates from the conflicting accounts of Aristophanes, Xenophon, and Plato. It details Socrates's unconventional lifestyle, his rejection of traditional teaching methods in favor of probing question-and-answer dialogues, and his relentless focus on examining the state of people's souls.

principles.statement: The unexamined life is not worth living for a human being.
principles.rationale: Socrates dedicated his life to questioning people about what matters most, such as courage, love, and the state of their souls, believing that continuous self-examination is essential to human existence.
principles.source_quote: the unexamined life is not worth living for a human being

principles.statement: I am not a teacher who transmits information.
principles.rationale: Socrates rejected the idea that knowledge could be passively transmitted from teacher to student, instead helping others discover and recognize truth on their own.
principles.source_quote: Socrates adamantly insisted he was not a teacher

principles.rationale: Through Socratic irony, he approached conversations claiming ignorance, which allowed him to listen to others while systematically probing and testing their beliefs.
principles.source_quote: saying that he knew nothing of importance and wanted to listen to others

frameworks.name: Elenctic Questioning
frameworks.when_to_apply: When examining people's lives, their beliefs about virtues (courage, love, reverence), and the state of their souls.
steps: Engage a conversation partner in a question-and-answer mode.
Adopt a stance of irony, claiming to know nothing of importance.
Question the partner to confuse, sting, and stun them into realizing their own ignorance.
Use this state of realized ignorance to spark genuine intellectual curiosity and help them recognize what is real, true, and good on their own.
frameworks.source_quote: confusing, stinging and stunning his conversation partners into the unpleasant experience of realizing their own ignorance, a state sometimes superseded by genuine intellectual curiosity.

mental_models.name: The Daimonion (Internal Voice)
mental_models.description: An internal, semi-divine voice that acts as a negative constraint, prohibiting Socrates from doing certain things (both trivial and important) rather than prescribing what he should do.
mental_models.example: Socrates acknowledged a rather strange personal phenomenon, a daimonion or internal voice that prohibited his doing certain things, some trivial and some important

mental_models.name: Education as Self-Recognition (Anti-Pitcher Model)
mental_models.description: A lens for viewing education not as a transaction of information, but as a guided process of internal discovery. Teachers are not pitchers pouring contents into empty cups.
mental_models.example: Because Socrates was no transmitter of information that others were passively to receive, he resists the comparison to teachers. Rather, he helped others recognize on their own what is real, true, and good

heuristics: Question anyone you meet—young or old, rich or poor—about what matters most.
Never take money for philosophical conversations.
Listen to your internal voice (daimonion) when it prohibits an action.

signature_quotes.text: the unexamined life is not worth living for a human being
signature_quotes.context: Stated at his trial, explaining his single-minded dedication to examining his own and others' lives.

signature_quotes.text: saying that he knew nothing of importance and wanted to listen to others
signature_quotes.context: A description of his characteristic irony during discussions.

anti_patterns.description: Treating students as empty cups to be filled with information.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It assumes knowledge can be passively received rather than requiring the individual to recognize what is real, true, and good on their own.

anti_patterns.description: Prioritizing fame, wealth, honors, and political power over the state of one's soul.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leads to an unexamined life, neglecting the improvement of the soul and the pursuit of what truly matters.
